channel.title: FT – Fiction
channel.description: Fiction reviews and articles from the Financial Times.

item.title: The best books of the week
item.description: From the crash of 1873 to our ‘might is right’ world order and a fan’s paean to George Michael. Plus dinosaurs, Andrew Sean Greer’s new novel, short stories by Ruth Ozeki — and Barry Forshaw’s pick of crime fiction

item.title: The Typing Lady — short stories distil the essence of Ruth Ozeki’s fiction
item.description: An ambitious and playful first collection from the novelist ranges from the auto-fictional to the surreal

item.title: Villa Coco — Andrew Sean Greer piles on the charm
item.description: The Pulitzer winner’s latest novel builds a richly quirky world, at times excessively so, around a friendship in Tuscany

item.title: From Patricia Cornwell’s memoir to Simenon and a whodunnit at sea
item.description: A crop of top crime titles feature a professional housebreaker, a troubled detective, a determined lawyer and a French classic

item.title: What are your favourite books of 2026 so far?
item.description: This year’s much-anticipated Summer Books series is almost upon us — and, as ever, we want to hear from you

item.title: Ambivalence by Brian Dillon — a ‘coming-to-be’ memoir of ideas
item.description: Covering the writer’s late teens and early twenties, this pleasurable read is an elegant account of self-development through art

item.title: Land — Maggie O’Farrell’s ambitious novel of family, Ireland and empire
item.description: The acclaimed ‘Hamnet’ author revisits themes of strife and loss but on an epic canvas spanning centuries and continents

item.title: My five lessons from judging this year’s International Booker Prize
item.description: Reading 128 books in eight months was a privilege — and a lasting education in making time, embracing difference and building literary muscle

item.title: The Home of the Drowned — a saga of Sámi people and the battle to preserve their past
item.description: Elin Anna Labba’s powerful fiction debut explores memory, marginalisation and what happens when collective wisdom is washed away by modernity

item.title: AI stories aren’t inevitably ‘not art’
item.description: Exercising our own judgment when it comes to quality is something we should not outsource to machines

item.title: A puzzling death in London and a tradwife’s downfall — the best new audio books
item.description: Twists, turns and revelations feature in the tragic loss of a young man in ‘London Falling’, while ‘Yesteryear’ is one of this summer’s most talked-about debuts

item.title: The Left and the Lucky — Willy Vlautin’s hymn to blue-collar American grit
item.description: From an author who pens songs of masculinity and working-class strife comes a pitch-perfect novel that may be his finest yet

item.title: Interview.International Booker Prize winner Yáng Shuāng-zi: ‘We still live under a colonial regime’
item.description: The writer, along with her translator Lin King, on using the power of fiction to bring Taiwan out of the shadow of China

item.title: Whistler by Ann Patchett — coming to terms with the past
item.description: The American author’s 10th novel is a compassionate portrait of a blended family in the aftermath of an unexpected reunion

item.title: Yáng Shuāng-zi and Lin King win the International Booker Prize
item.description: A historical tale of travel, power and romance is the first Taiwanese novel to win the influential award for fiction in translation

item.title: Autobiography of Cotton by Cristina Rivera Garza — truths told through fact and fiction
item.description: The Pulitzer-winning author weaves an account of a real-life cotton workers’ strike in Mexico in the 1930s with her own family history, memoir and invented tales

item.title: Interview.Douglas Stuart: ‘I write to make sense of my nation’
item.description: The ‘Shuggie Bain’ author about his struggles growing up in Scotland and new novel ‘John of John’ that is packed with themes of love, sexuality, class and inheritance

item.title: Uprising — Tahmima Anam’s novel of rebellion is her finest to date
item.description: Seen through children’s eyes, this fable of oppression draws on a real-life ‘floating brothel’ in Bangladesh, as well as the country’s revolutions

item.title: Hunger & Thirst — Claire Fuller goes darker
item.description: A 1980s-set tale of childhood trauma and female friendship explores the unsettling space between real and imagined terrors

item.title: Wash by Erica Wagner — the man behind the Brooklyn Bridge
item.description: A poignant fictional account of the life of Washington Roebling and the engineering feat that dominates the New York cityscape

item.title: Dark is the Morning by Rupert Thomson — a question of infidelity
item.description: An Abruzzo-set tale about a man’s psychological unravelling is a surprising, skilfully wrought novel for a long, sweltering afternoon

item.title: Best new thrillers — five pacy tales of money, mafiosi and betrayal
item.description: A Sopranos-style story of drugs and family in Bogotá, an interpreter finds that a little knowledge is a dangerous thing, Bond is back — and more

item.title: Little Vanities — Sarah Gilmartin’s novel of unravelling friendships
item.description: Over shifting timelines, the Irish author’s third novel unpicks the fracturing relationships of a group of thirty-somethings

item.title: The Last Movement — a beautiful portrait of Mahler, a genius all at sea
item.description: Robert Seethaler’s gem of a novella mixes the dying composer’s reflections with real-life history and imaginative flourishes

item.title: Hey Man by Andrew Meehan — from bromance to the brotherhood
item.description: A lonely teenager lodges with his father’s cousin in this story of substitute love and male friendship
